learning_objectives: Develop a cross-platform strategy that provides appropriate experiences for each device context
Decide what features belong on which platforms based on user context and device capabilities
Balance platform consistency with platform-appropriate design
readings: Multiscreen UX Design by Wolfram Nagel, Ch. 1-3
Responsive Design Patterns by Ethan Marcotte, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Cross-platform design is not about making the same thing everywhere. It is about providing the right experience on the right device at the right moment — leveraging each platform's unique strengths.
key_concepts: Platform strengths: mobile (portability, sensors, camera), desktop (screen space, precision input, multitasking), tablet (reading, drawing), watch (glanceable, notifications)
Device-shifting patterns: how users move between devices during a single task
Content parity vs. feature parity: all content accessible but features may differ
Responsive, adaptive, and platform-native approaches
Shared design systems across platforms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping a cross-platform user journey for a project management tool — showing which features belong on mobile, desktop, and tablet based on usage context

review.value: Users start tasks on one device and finish on another — researching on phone, purchasing on desktop. Cross-platform design must ensure seamless state handoff between devices.
All content should be accessible on all platforms. But features may differ: mobile gets camera-based input, desktop gets advanced editing, tablet gets drawing tools. Features match device strengths.

learning_objectives: Build design systems that work across web, iOS, and Android
Define platform-agnostic design tokens and platform-specific components
Manage design system variants for different platforms and screen sizes
readings: Design Systems by Alla Kholmatova, Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: A cross-platform design system shares foundations (tokens, principles) while allowing platform-specific component implementations. The goal is brand consistency without forcing platform uniformity.
key_concepts: Shared foundations: design tokens, grid system, typography scale, color palette
Platform-specific components: iOS switches vs. Android toggles, native pickers
Token translation: Figma variables to CSS custom properties to Swift/Kotlin values
Platform detection: adapting component behavior based on platform capabilities
Documentation for multiple platforms: showing variants side-by-side
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a cross-platform card component in Figma — shared structure and tokens with iOS, Android, and web variants showing platform-appropriate interactions
setup_requirements: Platform guidelines reference (Apple HIG, Material Design)

review.value: Color tokens, typography scale, spacing grid, and design principles are universal. Component implementations adapt to platform conventions — the same blue-500 token renders correctly on web (CSS), iOS (UIColor), and Android (Color resource).

Date pickers, action sheets, navigation patterns, and system fonts should follow platform conventions. Users expect iOS apps to feel like iOS apps — forcing Material Design onto iOS creates cognitive friction.

learning_objectives: Choose between responsive, adaptive, and platform-native approaches for each product context
Design adaptive layouts that fundamentally restructure for different form factors
Handle the tablet middle ground between phone and desktop
readings: Adaptive Web Design by Aaron Gustafson, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: The spectrum from responsive to adaptive to platform-native represents increasing design investment and increasing platform optimization. Choosing the right point on this spectrum depends on the product, users, and resources.
key_concepts: Responsive: fluid layout that flexes to any width using CSS
Adaptive: distinct layouts for specific breakpoints or device classes
Platform-native: purpose-built apps for each platform from scratch
The tablet challenge: too big for phone layout, too small for desktop
Progressive disclosure across screen sizes: more space reveals more features
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the same email client for phone, tablet, and desktop — showing responsive fluid behavior and adaptive layout shifts at key breakpoints

review.value: Tablets often use a split-view (list + detail) layout that combines the phone's list view with the desktop's detail view. This leverages the tablet's unique screen size rather than just stretching a phone layout.

As screen size increases, progressively reveal more features and information. Mobile shows essentials; tablet adds secondary actions; desktop shows the full feature set — all accessible but contextually appropriate.

learning_objectives: Design seamless state synchronization between platforms for multi-device users
Handle offline scenarios and sync conflicts across devices
Create handoff experiences that continue tasks across devices
readings: Designing Connected Products by Rowland et al., Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern users expect their data and progress to follow them across devices instantly. Designing for cross-device state sync means handling the reality of network latency, offline periods, and sync conflicts.
key_concepts: Real-time sync: changes on one device appear instantly on others
Eventual consistency: syncing when reconnected after offline use
Conflict resolution: what happens when two devices edit the same data offline
Handoff UX: 'Continue on your phone' from desktop and vice versa
Cross-device notifications: notifying the right device at the right time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the sync and conflict resolution experience for a notes app — handling offline edits on two devices, showing merge UI for conflicts, and testing the handoff flow

review.value: When two devices edit the same content offline, show the user both versions side-by-side with the differences highlighted and let them choose or merge. Never silently discard a user's edits.
Seamless handoff requires: (1) clear entry point on the receiving device, (2) instant state loading without re-authentication, (3) contextual restoration of exactly where the user left off.

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct cross-platform usability testing
Test device-shifting scenarios with real users on real devices
Evaluate cross-platform consistency and platform-appropriateness simultaneously
readings: Remote Research by Nate Bolt & Tony Tulathimutte, Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing cross-platform experiences requires multi-device test setups and scenarios that span devices. Single-platform testing misses the critical handoff moments where cross-platform experiences typically break down.
key_concepts: Multi-device test setup: providing participants with multiple devices
Device-shifting task scenarios: tasks that naturally span devices
Evaluating consistency: does the brand feel cohesive across platforms?
Evaluating appropriateness: does each platform feel native and natural?
Cross-platform analytics: tracking user journeys that span devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Planning and running a multi-device usability test — a participant starts a task on phone, continues on desktop, and checks results on tablet, with observers tracking handoff friction

review.value: A test task that naturally requires switching devices: 'While commuting, find a recipe on your phone. When you get home, open it on your tablet to follow while cooking.' This reveals real handoff friction.

The dual evaluation criteria for cross-platform: is the experience consistent enough to feel like the same product? Is it platform-appropriate enough to feel native on each device? Both must be true simultaneously.

questions.question: A product team debates whether to build one responsive web app or separate native apps for iOS and Android. What factors should drive this decision, and when is each approach appropriate?
questions.answer: Factors: (1) Platform-specific features needed — camera, GPS, push notifications, offline work favor native. (2) Performance requirements — high-performance graphics and animations favor native. (3) Development resources — one responsive web app costs less than two native apps. (4) User expectations — consumer apps often expect native feel while business tools may accept web. (5) Distribution — native apps require app store approval; web is instantly deployable. Responsive web is appropriate when: content-focused product, limited budget, rapid iteration needed, no platform-specific features required. Native apps appropriate when: performance-critical, heavy use of device sensors, offline-first requirement, users expect native interaction patterns.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies when responsive web is appropriate
Correctly identifies when native is appropriate

questions.question: Explain how a cross-platform design system handles a date picker component differently across web, iOS, and Android while maintaining brand consistency.
questions.answer: Brand consistency layer (shared): same color tokens, same typography scale, same label text, same validation rules, same error message format. These shared foundations ensure the date picker is recognizably part of the same product on every platform. Platform-specific layer: Web uses a custom dropdown calendar matching the design system. iOS uses the native UIDatePicker wheel (users expect it; fighting it creates friction). Android uses the Material Design date picker dialog. Each native picker is styled with brand colors where the platform allows customization, but the interaction pattern follows platform conventions. The result: the date picker looks like the same brand but feels native to each platform.

rubric.key: Explains the balance between consistency and platform-native feel

questions.question: A user edits a document on their laptop while offline, then opens the same document on their phone (which was also offline) and makes different edits. Both devices reconnect. Describe the UX for resolving this conflict.
questions.answer: When both devices reconnect: (1) Detect the conflict automatically and notify the user on both devices. (2) Show a clear conflict resolution screen with both versions side-by-side, differences highlighted in color. (3) Provide three options: Keep laptop version, Keep phone version, or Merge (showing a combined version with conflicts marked inline). (4) For merge, highlight each conflict point and let the user choose which version to keep for each section. (5) Save the resolution and sync the merged version to both devices. (6) Keep both original versions in version history so the user can recover either one later. Critical principle: never silently discard user edits. Always show what happened and let the user decide.

rubric.key: Shows side-by-side comparison with highlighted differences
Provides multiple resolution options including merge

questions.question: Write a cross-platform usability test task scenario that tests device-shifting behavior for a travel booking app.
questions.answer: Scenario: 'You are on your lunch break at work. Using your phone, find a hotel in Barcelona for a trip next month (March 15-20) for two adults. Browse options and save two hotels you like to your favorites. Later that evening, you are at home on your laptop. Open the travel app, find the hotels you saved on your phone, compare them side by side, and book the one you prefer. Tomorrow morning, check your phone for the booking confirmation and save it to your phone wallet.' This scenario tests: (1) mobile search and browse experience, (2) cross-device state sync (favorites saved on phone appear on desktop), (3) desktop comparison and booking experience, (4) handoff back to mobile for post-booking confirmation. Each device is used for what it does best.

rubric.key: Each device used for its strength

major_assignments.title: Cross-Platform Product Design
major_assignments.description: Design a complete cross-platform experience for a product spanning mobile, tablet, and desktop. Create a shared design system, platform-appropriate designs, and test the cross-platform experience with users on real devices.
objectives: Develop a cross-platform strategy defining what features belong where
Build a shared design system with platform-specific component variants
Design platform-appropriate experiences for phone, tablet, and desktop
Test cross-platform usability including device-shifting scenarios
deliverables: Cross-platform strategy document: feature matrix by platform
Shared design system: tokens, grid, typography with platform variants
High-fidelity designs: mobile (8+ screens), tablet (6+ screens), desktop (6+ screens)
Interactive prototypes for each platform's primary flow
Cross-platform usability test report with 3+ multi-device participants

criteria: Feature distribution is logical based on device context
Content parity ensured across platforms
Device-shifting scenarios identified and designed for
Strategy document is clear and well-reasoned
Tokens shared effectively across platforms
Platform-specific components follow native conventions
System is documented for multi-platform development
Consistency and appropriateness balanced
Each platform feels native and appropriate
Brand consistency maintained across platforms
Layouts leverage each platform's strengths
All screen states designed for each platform
Multi-device testing with real participants on real devices
Device-shifting scenarios tested explicitly
Handoff friction identified and addressed
Cross-platform consistency evaluated
Thoughtful cross-platform strategy with logical feature distribution
Shared design system with platform-appropriate variants
High-quality designs leveraging each platform's strengths
Multi-device usability testing with real participants

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on cross-platform strategy, design systems, and state management

platform_labs.description: Weekly labs designing and prototyping for multiple platforms

cross_platform_project.description: Complete cross-platform product design with shared system and testing

platform_critiques.description: Weekly analysis of cross-platform products evaluating consistency and appropriateness

final_presentation.description: Presentation of cross-platform project demonstrating all platform designs

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve multi-device prototyping that requires participation. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Platform deliverables build sequentially. Late work penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Design system foundations may be discussed collaboratively. All designs must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original. Using platform UI kits without significant modification is prohibited for graded work.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma with multi-platform frames, access to iOS and Android devices for testing, web browser for responsive testing, and screen recording software.
